item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there are plenty of skips that suggest all kinds of alterations to real-life people

Yeah, and I don't approve of any of them. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damien Hirst is alive at the time this article is written

Or is he? dun dun duuun

In all seriousnss, there are plenty of skips that suggest all kinds of alterations to real-life people and locations: the simplest solution I have to that is that they take place in alternate realities. This is no different in that regard. I appreciate that you took the time to check it out regardless of what you thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really gonna have to go back through series 4 when it's done, I don't know how I keep missing things. x.x

I really wish there were a date on this somewhere. The one thing killing suspension of disbelief for me is that Damien Hirst is alive at the time this article is written, real-world, and the fact that they can't locate him suggests this is actually him. I just don't know what to make of that.

I really like the idea of someone capitalizing on the whole "artist gains recognition after death" thing. The art anomalies were creative and the interview didn't overstay its welcome.

I went into this not knowing who Hirst was, and I assumed he was fictional :p Apparently he's a real dude, and ehhhh. Not too sure how I feel about that, makes the skip almost feel like a PSA/ad for him and his work, and I would probably like it more if the author invented a character for this skip, but it's not a dealbreaker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm seriously considering doing a Hirst cosplay after my finals, complete with low-budget recreations of his artworks.

That sounds amazing. :O If you ever do, please post some pictures!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I didn't mind having Bach name-dropped in there - Hirst was no music historian after all, and we can't expect him to know all the facts accurately. But it's okay.

I actually went to Google to see if those art installations were real, lol. For me at least, you've definitely succeeded with sparking interest in Damien Hirst! I'm seriously considering doing a Hirst cosplay after my finals, complete with low-budget recreations of his artworks.

Thank you for introducing me to Hirst, by the way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the other hand, I believe the article would be a lot better if it could include pictures of the art installations.

I'd have to disagree. My goals with this article were to bring attention towards Hirst's work whilst still telling a coherent and unsettling story. Simply giving a brief description of the installations piques curiosity- it makes people who haven't seen these pieces want to, if you see what I mean.

This isn't exactly true. Bach's keyboard works were widely circulated during his lifetime, and he also made a name for himself as a virtuoso organist.

Lack of research on my part- I'll see if I can find a fitting enough replacement. Thank you for the feedback and the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how there is a strong Hirstian aesthetic permeating the article. Hirst/SCP-3371 himself is also characterful, and I particularly liked the dialogue.

On the other hand, I believe the article would be a lot better if it could include pictures of the art installations.

A few more minor nitpicks:

all of which SCP-3371 claims to have created during their corporeal state.

Pronoun slip-up. Should be "its".

Bach kicked the fucking bucket before he was a household name.

This isn't exactly true. Bach's keyboard works were widely circulated during his lifetime, and he also made a name for himself as a virtuoso organist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So often anartists seem to be interested only in being right bastards. This pseudoHirst is really committed to the art itself. That merits respect.

I just now, during writing the first sentence, realized the connection between 'anartist' and 'anarchist'. I had never thought of that before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool. I love anart related SCPs! Here's my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTF Mu-13 ("Ghostbusters")

I'm liking this already.

And the rest of the article didn't really disappoint. I'm a little lost on how he turned brought about the anart, but then again he did manage to resurrect himself in an incorporeal state impervious to all physical harm, so maybe I should just be taking that with a grain of salt.

On the subject of the depiction of a modern artist committing suicide in the name of artistic spirit, I don't really know much about Hirst, so given the choice I don't really have much context on what he might do. It's mostly played as gallows humor, so I didn't really have much of a problem with it. For lack of a better word - a much better word - it's delightful, in its simplicity. You manage to get across a potent (albeit trod-upon) message within a relatively sparse length, and the imagery contained within the videolog presents a decently creepy counterpoint to the levity that pervades the piece.

+↑

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love that last line. Fantastic way to end the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed that interview log. That dialogue is great and I like it and would probably upvote for it alone (mainly since I didn't know/still don't quite know who Damien Hirst is).

+1 for that dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With special thanks to:
Zyn Roget Sly161
Boogey_Man23 Kenoma MaliceAforethought
LadyKatie and Zachary Maxwell for their help and support.
